policy_statement: Introduce demand-side minimum efficiency standards for lighting, appliances, motor vehicles, and street lighting (NEEAP initiative).
mechanism_description: New minimum efficiency standards for lighting, appliances, motor vehicles, and street lighting improve end-use efficiency and reduce electricity and fuel use, lowering associated emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Same service (lighting/mobility/appliance output) → ↓ energy input (electricity/fuels) → ↓ fossil combustion in grids/engines → ↓ GHG emissions
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: Regulators set set minimum energy performance standards (MEPS) that remove inefficient appliances, lamps, vehicles, and municipal streetlights from the market/procurement stream.  
(2) Mediating physical process: Higher end-use efficiency delivers the same lumen output, cooling/heating, or vehicle-km with fewer kWh or liters; this reduces upstream electricity generation and/or tailpipe fuel combustion.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower energy demand leads to reduced CO₂e emissions (and often co-emitted air pollutants) from power plants and transport fuel use.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced electricity and fuel consumption, leading to lower CO₂e emissions from power generation and direct fuel combustion.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Standards raise end-use efficiency, yielding the same service with less energy input, which then reduces combustion and GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While the ultimate benefit is mitigation (lower emissions), the defining mechanism is demand-side efficiency—i.e., delivering the same service with less energy—so the primary category is Resource Efficiency under the “same service → less energy” pathway.  
3) Edge cases applied: If standards target fuel switching (e.g., mandating EVs) or renewable supply, the dominant pathway could shift toward direct Mitigation via decarbonization rather than efficiency; however, this canonical mechanism is explicitly “minimum efficiency standards → energy use reduction.”  
4) Applicability to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any MEPS-type policy that improves end-use efficiency reduces energy consumption for the same service, so Resource Efficiency remains the correct primary category across appliances, lighting, vehicles, and municipal lighting.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Any further subtyping (e.g., buildings vs transport vs municipal operations; electricity vs fuel; co-benefits like air quality) depends on policy-specific evidence and is deferred to policy-level classification.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Vehicle standards can sometimes be framed as emissions-intensity standards or technology mandates; if the policy primarily drives fuel switching (e.g., zero-emission vehicle mandates) rather than efficiency, the dominant pathway may shift toward Mitigation. For street lighting, adaptive controls (dimming/smart systems) still fit the same “service maintained → less energy” pathway.
key_indicators: “Demand-side efficiency standards”; “new minimum standards for lighting, appliances, motor vehicles, and street lighting”
co_benefits: Improved air quality from reduced fuel/power combustion; consumer/municipal cost savings from lower energy bills; potential public health benefits
instance_edge_case_notes: Street lighting standards could also be implemented via procurement requirements, but the phrasing “new minimum standards” indicates a general MEPS/performance-standard instrument rather than procurement-specific language.
